skill.name: database-migration
skill.description: Plan and review database migrations across ORMs and platforms, including schema changes, data transformations, rollback strategies, and zero-downtime deployment steps.
skill.summary: Guides database schema and data migrations with rollback and zero-downtime planning.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown code fences as shell backtick execution and misread ordinary migration text as weak cryptography or reconnaissance. I found no evidence of prompt injection, malicious execution, network access, credential access, or exfiltration in SKILL.md. The remaining risk is operational: examples include destructive database migration steps that require review before use.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown code fences and commented migration commands, not executable skill logic. They are safe as documentation, but users should review commands before running migrations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are Markdown fences around JavaScript, TypeScript, and Prisma examples. The only CLI commands are comments that describe migration workflows.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines do not use MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithms. They contain the skill description, a database version upgrade bullet, and a Markdown resource path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic API or hash algorithm on these lines. The matches appear to be substring or file-extension confusion.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines are database schema and SQL example text, not host discovery or system enumeration. No commands collect operating system, network, or user environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 110 defines a Prisma id field, and line 228 is a SQL WHERE id clause. Neither line contains reconnaissance behavior.

low_findings.title: Destructive Migration Examples Require Review
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples that remove columns or drop tables as part of migration and rollback workflows. This is expected for the topic, but copying examples without backups or staging validation could cause data loss.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly show column removal and table drop operations. The risk is operational rather than malicious because these examples are normal migration patterns.

content.value_statement: Database migrations can cause data loss, downtime, or failed releases when steps are unclear. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan schema changes, data backfills, rollbacks, and zero-downtime releases.
actual_capabilities: Explains migration patterns for Sequelize, TypeORM, and Prisma.
Provides staged approaches for schema changes such as adding, renaming, and changing columns.
Outlines data transformation workflows with validation and rollback considerations.
Describes transaction-based and checkpoint-based rollback strategies.
Plans zero-downtime migration phases for backward-compatible deployments.
Highlights database dialect differences for cross-platform migrations.
limitations: It does not connect to or inspect a live database.
Examples must be adapted to the application's real schema and data volume.
It cannot guarantee zero downtime without deployment, traffic, and database constraints.
Rollback plans still require staging tests and current backups.

use_cases.title: Plan an ORM Migration
use_cases.description: Create an ordered migration plan for teams moving schema management between Sequelize, TypeORM, or Prisma.

use_cases.title: Review a Production Schema Change
use_cases.description: Check whether a proposed column rename, type change, or table update needs staged deployment steps.

use_cases.title: Prepare Rollback Procedures
use_cases.description: Define backup, validation, checkpoint, and rollback actions before a high-risk release.

prompt_templates.title: Describe a Simple Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a small schema change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a database migration that adds a required status field to an existing users table. Include validation and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review ORM Migration Safety
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of migration code and release steps.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my ORM migration plan for safety. Identify downtime risks, data loss risks, missing rollback steps, and staging checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Data Transformation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced migration with backfill and data cleanup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a migration plan to split a single address field into street, city, and state fields for a large production table.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Zero-Downtime Release Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced phased deployment across app and database changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a zero-downtime migration plan for renaming a heavily used column. Include app deployment phases, backfill, monitoring, and rollback.

output_examples.input: Plan a safe migration for renaming a users.name column to users.full_name.
output: Add the new column while keeping the old column available.
Deploy application code that writes both fields and reads the old field.
Backfill existing rows in batches and validate row counts.
Switch reads to the new field after validation.
Remove the old field only after rollback risk is accepted.

output_examples.input: Review a migration that drops a table during rollback.
output: Confirm a current backup exists before the release.
Check whether the table has foreign keys or active application reads.
Prefer a checkpoint table or restore procedure before destructive rollback.
Test rollback in staging with production-like data volume.

best_practices: Test every migration and rollback path in staging before production.
Use small, backward-compatible steps for high-traffic tables.
Take backups and define validation queries before destructive changes.
anti_patterns: Do not drop columns in the same release that removes application reads.
Do not run large backfills in one transaction without performance testing.
Do not assume rollback is safe when data has been transformed or deleted.

faq.question: Does this skill execute database migrations?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning and review guidance. Users must run commands in their own controlled environment.

faq.question: Which ORMs does it cover?
faq.answer: It includes examples for Sequelize, TypeORM, and Prisma, plus general database migration patterns.

faq.question: Can it guarantee zero downtime?
faq.answer: No. It can propose phased plans, but downtime depends on schema locks, traffic, deployment order, and database behavior.

faq.question: Is it safe for production databases?
faq.answer: It is safe as guidance, but production use requires backups, staging tests, monitoring, and human approval.

faq.question: Can it help with rollback plans?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains transaction-based rollback, checkpoint tables, validation steps, and restore considerations.

faq.question: Does it handle data transformations?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes migration patterns for reading existing records, transforming values, validating results, and cleaning old fields.
